title: Quantization stability & SLO tradeoffs - KV warmup, batching interaction, on-prem considerations
preview:
Meeting header: 2026-10-07 15:30 UTC | Duration: ~72 min | Attendees: Samir (Redwood AE), Ava (Redwood SE), Priya (Nimbus ML Infra), Marco (Nimbus SRE), Helen (Nimbus Eng Lead)

Summary (auto): technical deep dive on quantization-induced stability and SLO impact. Discussed KV cache warmup strategies, batching interactions that amplify apparent accuracy regressions after INT8/AWQ conversion, on-prem networking considerations, and a plan for a warmup + regression benchmark. Agreed to exchange prompt sets and run a 24-hour warmup test.

00:00 Samir (Redwood AE): Alright everyone, thanks for joining — we'll run about an hourish deep dive on the quantization artifacts you saw, focusing on KV cache warmup, batching, and how that shows up in on-prem setups. Ava will lead the bench details.
00:22 Ava (Redwood SE): Hi hi, thanks. Quick ask before we start: Priya, can you confirm the model variant you're running? You mentioned a 7B variant but I want the exact— is it the community gpt-7b-xl or the custom-tuned fork?
00:36 Priya (Nimbus): It's the gpt-seven-b fork with a tiny finetune for medical parsing. Sorry my shorthand, it's the -med-tune variant.
00:44 Ava: Cool, that matters because kernel selection and quant mapping can differ a bit. Also are you on TF32/FP16 baseline or pure FP32 currently?
00:56 Priya: Baseline was FP16 on our on-prem A100s, we tried INT8 via gptq and then AWQ as an alternative. The first thing we noticed was sporadic hallucination spikes on longer prompts.
01:14 Samir: Got it. Could you describe the workload pattern — short chatty requests vs long document decoding?
01:22 Marco (Nimbus SRE): Mostly multi-turn, often warm-started from stored prefixes, so KV cache reuse is common. Peak QPS is moderate — 45-60 reqs/s — but tail latency is critical: 95th under 200ms.
01:38 Ava: That tail SLO is helpful. We often see quantization regressions amplified at tail due to batching and stale KV cache interactions. I'll sketch that out now.
02:05 Ava: (screen share ref) Simplified flow: request arrives -> router picks GPU -> runtime may batch -> KV cache hit/miss -> decode. If quant change shifts logits slightly, early tokens can cause cache divergence — leading to different cached states and compounded error.
02:35 Priya: Sorry, explain \"cache divergence\" again? We thought KV cache was just bytes saved, why would quantization change that?
02:44 Ava: Totally. So KV cache stores per-step keys/values. If the quantized model produces different hidden activations even by small epsilon, the keys/values differ. Downstream, when those cached tokens are reused as prefix, the newer values are not bit-identical, so their contribution changes, and errors can accumulate over a long decode.
03:05 Priya: Okay that makes sense, small numeric shift cascades.
03:12 Samir: Also worth noting kernel ordering and rounding differences across A100 drivers — sometimes the same quant file behaves slightly differently across driver versions.
03:26 Marco: We had one node that showed better accuracy than others; I assumed hardware flakiness. Could driver mismatch be the cause?
03:34 Ava: Absolutely. We've seen mismatched cuBLAS/cuDNN/kernel selection cause tiny differences. We add a checksum step in our deploy to detect cross-node differences.
03:50 Helen (Nimbus): On batching — we use dynamic batching to keep latency down. Is there any rule-of-thumb how batch size interacts with INT8/AWQ?
04:03 Ava: Two effects: first, effective sequence alignment inside batch can cause different per-step compute paths. Second, batching increases the chance a request will read a KV entry written under a different numeric regime if you're mixing model variants. For pure single-variant deploys, larger batches mainly affect latency, not accuracy, but they can amplify timing jitter which sometimes looks like accuracy noise.
04:30 Priya: When you say \"different numeric regime\" do you mean we might mix fp16 and int8 in routing?
04:37 Samir: Yes, in hybrid setups we route to fallbacks or canaries that might be quantized differently. That mixed traffic is a big vector for apparent regression.
04:48 Marco: We do have canary routing during rollout, but we didn't realize it could cause cache contamination.
04:55 Ava: Exactly — if a canary writes keys to a shared KV layer or if sticky routing isn't strict, subsequent requests hitting another variant will see the stale keys. In Redwood private we isolate KV per variant or use per-instance caches to avoid this.
05:20 Priya: Our on-prem config is shared NVMe-backed cache to save memory, so it's shared across pods. Hmm.
05:29 Ava: That explains a lot. Shared persistent caches are higher risk unless you version the cache key namespace with model id+quant tag.
05:40 Samir: Quick aside on warmup: we recommend a multi-phase warmup. Short 1-2 hour synthetic warmup is often insufficient. The patterns you care about — long multi-turn prefixes — take longer to populate and surface staleness.
06:00 Priya: We did a 2 hour warmup and thought we were fine. You're suggesting 24 hours?
06:07 Ava: At scale, yes. Especially if you have a low-frequency long-tail prompt. We propose: 1) synthetic warmup to exercise kernels; 2) replay of production prefixes for 24h to populate the KV. That surface regressions that only appear after repeated reuse.
06:26 Marco: Sound doable. Does that require holding GPU memory for the KV longer? We worry about memory pressure for multi-tenant nodes.
06:34 Ava: You can use sticky cache populations: replay to each node and then GC older entries; or use a compact KV with truncation policy tuned to your prefix length distribution. Redwood's runtime supports max-kv-entries per model instance and LRU/TTL policies.
06:52 Priya: About AWQ vs GPTQ — we tried AWQ after GPTQ looked noisy. AWQ reduced some hallucinations but introduced a different slowness pattern. Is that expected?
07:05 Ava: Yes. AWQ uses asymmetric quantization which can preserve ranges better but may change rounding behavior — that can alter softmax dynamics, which can improve certain probabilities but also shift token ordering in edge cases. GPTQ sometimes underestimates outlier weights and can bias toward safer tokens but lose nuance. There's no one-size-fits-all.
07:35 Samir: Also inference kernel choices differ — AWQ gets a different optimized kernel path that may have slightly higher latency per token. We can show kernel-level traces.
07:48 Marco: If AWQ adds latency, that could push your 95th over 200ms in our traffic. Any mitigations?
07:57 Ava: Several: 1) micro-batching (we use continuous batching with dynamic max batch size), 2) prefix caching to avoid recomputing earlier layers, 3) model pipelining or using faster kernel for early layers and AWQ for later layers — hybrid precision. But hybrid introduces complexity in correctness.
08:25 Priya: Hybrid precision — do you mean fp16 for first N layers, int8 for last layers?
08:33 Ava: Correct. Often earlier layers contain more signal, later layers can be quantized more aggressively. But you have to maintain KV key format compatibility. If your keys are created by mixed-precision runs, reuse must be consistent.
08:49 Priya: That's scary for us. We're constrained to deterministic outputs for regulatory reasons in some workflows.
08:56 Samir: For regulatory paths, we suggest canarying with deterministic checksums and automated rollback. Also document the acceptable delta thresholds for token distributions.
09:10 Helen: On deterministic checksums — do you calc over logits or final tokens?
09:15 Ava: Both. We do a per-token logit-delta histogram and a final-token-match rate. For your use case, final-token-match plus key-checksums for KV should be required.
09:27 Marco: You mentioned earlier that driver versions can shift kernels. How strict should our infra be?
09:33 Ava: Pin GPU driver, CUDA, and runtime libs across nodes for production. We recommend a canary pool with the exact same stack and periodic checksum runs across nodes to catch drift.
09:45 Priya: Question on batching: we observed that when we moved from batch size 1 to 4, some prompts suddenly regressed. Should we just keep batch size 1?
09:55 Ava: Not necessarily. Try to align sequence lengths inside batch (bucketing) to reduce padding-induced artifacts and use per-request sequence-aware caching. Also enable per-request latency caps — Redwood supports tail-targeted batching where requests close to SLO avoid joining big batches.
10:18 Samir: Another gotcha: random seed for sampling. If your eval uses nucleus sampling with temperature, small logit shifts change sample results dramatically. For deterministic evals, use greedy decode or fixed seeds where possible.
10:35 Priya: Our golden set uses stochastic decodes for nuance, so we can't just switch. We need an eval that mirrors production sampling.
10:44 Ava: Then compute distributional metrics (KL divergence, ngram overlap) and track changes rather than per-example token equality. That reduces noise sensitivity.
10:56 Marco: On on-prem networking — we expect 2ms ± jitter inside cluster, but occasionally nodes hit 10-20ms for NVMe reads. Does that matter for KV?
11:06 Ava: It does. If your KV is NVMe-backed and shared over network, latency spikes create asynchronous staleness windows. Those windows combined with batching can lead to a request reading slightly older keys than expected. For SLOs under 200ms, aim for sub-5ms stable KV access or local in-memory caches per instance with periodic persistence.
11:30 Priya: That's useful. Any suggestions for persistent cache namespace versioning?
11:36 Ava: Prefix cache keys with model-id:quant-tag:kernel-hash:deployment-id. That way, mismatched kernels or quant tags never reuse another variant's keys.
11:50 Priya: Good. We'll need that change.
11:54 Samir: Quick break — we'll pivot to the proposed benchmark plan. Ava?
12:00 Ava: Plan: 1) Baseline FP16 steady-state run with your production prompts to capture golden outputs. 2) Apply GPTQ and AWQ variants, run an isolated 24h replay of real prefixes across nodes to populate KV. 3) During replay, measure per-token logit deltas, final token divergence, p95/p99 latency, and kernel-level timings. 4) Run a staggered warmdown to see if regressions persist after GC.
12:34 Marco: For the 24h replay, can we limit to a subset of prefixes to reduce load?
12:39 Ava: Yes, pick the heavy-weight long-tail prefixes and high-risk prompt families. The goal is to replicate reuse patterns. Priya, that's where your example prompt sets come in.
12:51 Priya: We'll assemble three buckets: short chatty, long-form multi-turn medical case, and adversarial edge cases. We'll include golden outputs for each.
13:03 Samir: On measurement: we'll provide a small script to compute per-step KL and token-match, plus a dashboard with kernel traces. We'll also surface a simple signal: consecutive-run regression count (how many examples show worse metric vs baseline).
13:19 Helen: About staging: we prefer not to hit production GPUs for this. Should we spin a mirror cluster?
13:26 Ava: For best fidelity, mirror cluster with same driver stack is ideal. If cost is a constraint, we can run sequential replays on each node in smaller batches but that lengthens wall time.
13:38 Marco: We'll try to provision a 4-node mirror.
13:42 Priya: Any fast wins to try before the full run?
13:46 Ava: Yes: 1) Namespace the KV to prevent cross-variant contamination. 2) Pin drivers. 3) Run a 6h focused replay on the long-form bucket and inspect KL deltas — often that surfaces the major issues.
14:03 Samir: Let's capture the potential root cause matrix: numeric precision changes, kernel selection, KV cross-variant contamination, batching alignment, NVMe network jitter, and sampling stochasticity. We'll rank them by probability once we see the trace.
14:18 Priya: One more question: do you have an existing tool for per-token logit histograms? Or do we need to build?
14:25 Ava: Redwood has an eval agent that can instrument per-layer logits and produce histograms; we'll provide a minimal config to run it and a small collector script. We'll attach to the follow-up.
14:36 Marco: Security question — for on-prem pilot, how will Redwood access our cluster? We prefer SSH jump with restricted keys, no open inbound.
14:44 Samir: We support agent-based connectors or a temporary bastion. For sensitive clusters we can do air-gapped data exchange: you run the benchmark and upload the logs to a secure bucket for us to analyze. We'll share the security checklist after this call.
14:59 Helen: KMS integration — we use in-house HSM. Is that supported for model encryption or just key wrapping for artifacts?
15:06 Ava: We support KMS/HSM integration for key wrapping of model artifacts and for traffic to private control plane in Redwood Private. For on-prem, we can integrate via standard PKCS11/KMS adapters. We'll add docs.
15:20 Priya: Great.
15:25 Samir: Okay we are nearing the end. Quick recap: Redwood will run the proposed warmup + 24h replay on a mirror cluster (Ava), Nimbus will share prompt sets and provision mirror nodes (Priya/Marco), Samir will send the security and topology checklist and price ballpark. We'll reconvene with benchmark results mid-next week.
15:45 Helen: One tiny nit — when you run the bench, can you also include a run with pinned seed greedy decode so we can separate sampling noise from systematic drift?
15:53 Ava: Yep, we'll include greedy and stochastic modes.
15:57 Marco: And can you log kernel version/hash with each result?
16:01 Ava: Yes, kernel and driver checksum will be attached.
16:05 Priya: Thanks. That was useful.
16:09 Samir: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
16:12 (end)

Action items and artifacts referenced: 
- Redwood benchmark plan doc (link): https://redwood-internal/example-benchmarks/kv-warmup-plan (redwood shared link)
- Security checklist (will be sent): includes KMS adapters, PKCS11 notes, required ports and bastion setup
- Eval scripts: redwood-eval-agent v2.3 (attached in CRM record)

Notes on transcription: few speaker label swaps at 03:00-03:20 where automated system labeled Samir as Speaker 1; some technical tokens (AWQ/GPTQ/drivers) mis-transcribed in spots as AQW/GPP-TQ.

title: Security review: EU/US region pinning + KMS routing - Nimbus Analytics
preview: Customer is evaluating Redwood for EU-sensitive analytics workflows. Primary concerns: firm data residency in EU, customer-managed keys, explicit subprocessor list and cross-border fallbacks, audit log retention and access via RBAC. Discussed Redwood Private and Dedicated options, KMS models (hosted vs customer-managed HSM), and contractual controls (DPA + SCCs). Agreed to run an EU-pin throughput/latency benchmark and exchange legal/security artifacts.
text: Customer is evaluating Redwood for EU-sensitive analytics workflows. Primary concerns: firm data residency in EU, customer-managed keys, explicit subprocessor list and cross-border fallbacks, audit log retention and access via RBAC. Discussed Redwood Private and Dedicated options, KMS models (hosted vs customer-managed HSM), and contractual controls (DPA + SCCs). Agreed to run an EU-pin throughput/latency benchmark and exchange legal/security artifacts.

title: Runtime tuning workshop: sparse routing, ROPE scaling & KV strategies
preview: Extended technical deep-dive with Nimbus to scope runtime optimizations for hybrid Llama/Mistral/Qwen workloads with a Sparse (MoE) experiment path. Key outcomes: agreed to run a mixed-load benchmark, test reduced ROPE delta for long-context drift, prototype KV sharding, and validate quantized variants for throughput gains. Security and VPC constraints noted; next step deliverables and a pilot timeline established.
text: Extended technical deep-dive with Nimbus to scope runtime optimizations for hybrid Llama/Mistral/Qwen workloads with a Sparse (MoE) experiment path. Key outcomes: agreed to run a mixed-load benchmark, test reduced ROPE delta for long-context drift, prototype KV sharding, and validate quantized variants for throughput gains. Security and VPC constraints noted; next step deliverables and a pilot timeline established.
